Editable working document for keynote development
Who Will Ultimately Control the AI Infrastructure that Higher Education Is Coming to Depend On?
Deep research dossier and keynote drafting pack
Prepared as a full briefing pack: conference reading, abstract stress-test, annotated concept notebook, evidence base, policy agenda, slide storyboard, and Q&A backup.
Core framing The conference is asking how higher education should use AI. This keynote asks who will control the infrastructure on which those uses depend.

Conference
Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026, Teesside University
Conference dates
15–17 April 2026
Programme slot
Day 1 keynote, 1:30–2:15 pm, immediately before the panel on reimagining the purpose and promise of universities
Purpose
To give you a full set of notes, definitions, evidence, cautions, policy levers, slide architecture, and Q&A backup for drafting the keynote
Last updated
17 March 2026

Use this pack as raw material. It is not a finished script; it is a deliberately over-complete set of arguments, evidence, examples, cautions, and speaking lines you can cut down into a 45-minute keynote.

Contents
• 1. Executive synthesis
• 2. Reading the room: conference themes, programme tensions, and audience implications
• 3. Stress-testing your abstract
• 4. Annotated concept notebook: every note, definition, and linked idea expanded
• 5. Evidence base for the keynote
• 6. Strategic synthesis: the position your keynote can own
• 7. Practical policy agenda for universities
• 8. Suggested slide architecture and speaking notes
• 9. Backup Q&A
• 10. Coverage map of your original notes and selected sources
This dossier is designed to be worked with quickly: every main section ends with implications for the talk, and the later sections translate the research into slides, lines, and likely answers.

1. Executive synthesis
The conference programme is rich in pedagogy, assessment, ethics, literacy, and practice. Your keynote can do something different and complementary: make the infrastructure question explicit. The strongest version of the talk is not anti-AI and not anti-industry. It is anti-passivity. It argues that universities are moving from occasional tool use to structural dependence, and that once AI is infrastructural the key question shifts from capability to control, accountability, resilience, and bargaining power.
The central strategic move is to reframe adoption as governance. Instead of asking only whether a model is impressive, cheap, or convenient, the talk asks who shapes the defaults built into teaching, assessment, advising, analytics, and institutional decision-making. That reframe allows you to connect seemingly separate topics in the programme—assessment redesign, learner analytics, AI literacy, ethics, accessibility, vendor platforms, and institutional strategy—into one coherent argument.
The talk works best as a three-step argument. First, AI is already becoming invisible infrastructure in higher education: student use is mainstream, assessment has already changed, and tools are being stitched into everyday workflows. Second, the infrastructure layer is highly concentrated, capital-intensive, and increasingly controlled by external firms whose incentives are not identical to the public mission of universities. Third, lock-in is happening now, while standards, contracts, norms, integrations, and expectations are still fluid. This is the small window in which universities can still move from passive consumption to active stewardship.
What the room most needs from this keynote Your keynote should sound like a governance keynote for a room that has mostly been invited to think about pedagogy, productivity, and assessment. That difference is your advantage. It lets you connect the whole programme rather than compete with the sessions already focused on tools.
Three claims worth making clearly and early
• AI in higher education is no longer just a set of optional tools. It is becoming part of the substrate through which teaching, assessment, support, and governance are delivered.
• Infrastructure matters because it shapes what institutions can see, do, audit, export, contest, and change. Control does not have to mean owning every component, but it does require legibility, bargaining power, portability, and evaluation capacity.
• The practical choice is not between total in-house sovereignty and total outsourcing. It is between institutions that remain capable stewards of their AI stack and institutions that become captive customers inside an extractive ecosystem.
The overall tone to strike
• Serious, practical, and public-interest oriented rather than apocalyptic.
• Critical of s
... (truncated)

17626:        "transcript": "And as an example, again, for Mernhart, as a teaching and learning-focused academic, when we have set tools, the pedagogy starts to bend around those tools and what a platform can do and what a platform can measure. You can see this when we're using Moodle or Blackboard, you know, there used to be perhaps many more different, varied, weird and wonderful types of assessment, but when it has to be collapses, now there are different benefits to a VLE versus pen and paper and where before, but there is that standardization pressure and using these tools will only allow certain things. We should have that exposure, be able to pick the best thing, right? Have the right size model. We shouldn't be using just what's given to us, be it some frankly crappy co-pilot model or some huge frontier model that's very wasteful. We should decide what's right for us, and we should experiment and have the ability to plug and play with the different models that are given by providers or as time goes on and the open source models become much more prevalent. 53-What the conference is explicitly about. Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026 is framed as a multi-day gathering on the opportunities, challenges, and future directions of AI in higher education. The conference pages foreground AI-enhanced pedagogy, research and innovation, responsible AI and digital ethics, and rethinking assessment in the age of AI. The call for contributions broadens this into pedagogy and assessment, learning analytics and data use, equity and inclusion, student voice, institutional strategy, and EdTech practice.
54:What the programme is practically about. The scheduled sessions show a room preoccupied with assessment, writing, feedback, student use, practical integration, and institutional adaptation. Mini-glossary
Term
Working definition for the keynote
Invisible infrastructure
Systems so embedded in ordinary practice that they disappear from view until they fail, become contested, or shape behaviour in unwanted ways.
Path dependence
The way early choices and reinforcing feedback make some future options easier and others harder.
Stewardship
The active governance of systems so they remain legible, evaluable, negotiable, and aligned with mission.
Grounding
The extent to which an AI system is connected to the relevant world, context, data, and task constraints rather than only generic language patterns.
Evaluation
Structured testing of whether a system works adequately and safely for the tasks and values that matter in context.
Extractivism
The logic of taking value from people, environments, or institutions without reciprocal stewardship or fair return.
Surveillance capitalism
A regime in which human experience and behaviour are captured as data and converted into prediction and influence.
Hyperscaler
A very large cloud and infrastructure provider operating at a scale far beyond ordinary enterprise or public-sector actors.
Lock-in
A condition in which switching away from a system becomes costly because of technical, contractual, social, or organisational dependencies.
Source note

Here’s a draft you can send.

One quick note first: your materials consistently use `Dr Stuart Grey`, while one auto-transcript says `Gray`. I’d use `Grey`.

Thanks, that would be very helpful. Here’s some wording you’re welcome to use or adapt:

**How I’d like to be introduced**
Dr Stuart Grey has a background in distributed AI systems, teaches part-time at the University of Glasgow, and is the founder of Student Voice AI, a spin-out working with universities across the UK to analyse free-text student feedback. His work sits at the intersection of technical AI, teaching and learning, and higher education strategy.

**How to introduce the talk**
Stuart’s keynote asks a slightly different question from many current AI discussions in higher education: not just how universities should use AI, but who will control the AI infrastructure they are increasingly coming to depend on. The talk looks at what that means for governance, procurement, data, resilience, and student interests.

**A short explanation of Student Voice AI**
Student Voice AI is a spin-out that analyses free-text student comments for universities across the UK, including Russell Group institutions. In practice, that means using AI to help institutions make sense of large volumes of qualitative student feedback, while staying grounded in the real workflows, pressures, and governance questions universities are dealing with.

**A couple of possible questions for the end**
1. If a university wanted to act on this tomorrow, what is one practical step it should take first?
2. What do you think universities should retain control over, rather than outsource by default?
3. What rights should students have as AI becomes more embedded in teaching, assessment, and support?

If you want, I can tighten this into a more polished email reply or make it sound more formal / warmer / more concise.
